elements.sent-2411-001: We construct an uncountable subring of $\mathbb R$ with Hausdorff dimension zero (and hence of Lebesgue measure zero).
elements.sent-2411-002: We denote by $\mathbb N_0$ the set of non-negative integers, by $\mathbb N$ the set of positive integers and by $\mathbb Z$ the set of integers.. Let \begin{equation}S:=\{0\}\cup \{2^n : n\in \natn_0\}=\{0,1,2,2^2,2^3,\ldots\}.\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-003: For every $n\in\mathbb N$ by $nS$, we denote the $n^\text{th}$ sumset of $S$, i.e., \begin{equation}nS:=\{s_1+\cdots +s_n: s_1,\ldots,s_n\in S\}.\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-004: For every $n\in \mathbb{N}$ we define \begin{equation}A_n:=\left\{\sum\limits_{k\in nS} \frac{x_k}{2^k}: (x_k)_{k\in nS} is a bounded sequence of integers\right\}.\end{equation}.
elements.formula-2411-003: \begin{equation}A_n:=\left\{\sum\limits_{k\in nS} \frac{x_k}{2^k}: (x_k)_{k\in nS} is a bounded sequence of integers\right\}.\end{equation}
elements.sent-2411-005: The set $A_n$ is an uncountable subgroup of $\rear$.
elements.sent-2411-006: We first show that $A_n$ is a subgroup of $\rear$.
elements.sent-2411-007: Trivially, $0\in A_n$ and $-x\in A_n$ whenever $x\in A_n$.
elements.sent-2411-008: Thus, it suffices to show that $A_n$ is closed under addition.. Let $x,y\in A_n$.
elements.sent-2411-009: Therefore, \begin{equation}x=\sum\limits_{k\in nS} \frac{x_k}{2^k} \quad and \quad y=\sum\limits_{k\in nS} \frac{y_k}{2^k}\end{equation} where $(x_k)_{k\in nS}$ and $(y_k)_{k\in nS}$ are bounded sequences of integers.
elements.sent-2411-010: It follows that \begin{equation}x+y=\sum\limits_{k\in nS} \frac{x_k+y_k}{2^k}\end{equation} is also an element of $A_n$.
elements.sent-2411-011: Clearly, the set $A_n$ is uncountable, because by the uniqueness of binary expansions which do not end with an infinite string of $1$'s, the set of real numbers of the form \begin{equation}\sum\limits_{k\in nS} \frac{z_k}{2^k} \quad with z_k\in \{0,1\}\end{equation} is an uncountable subset of $A_n$.
elements.sent-2411-012: We now proceed to show that the set $A_n$ has Hausdorff dimension zero for every $n\in\natn$.
elements.sent-2411-013: For every $n\in\natn$ write \begin{equation}A_n:=\bigcup_{t=1}^{\infty} A_{n,t}\end{equation} where for every $t\in\natn$ we let \begin{equation}A_{n,t}:=\left\{\sum\limits_{k\in nS} \frac{x_k}{2^k}\in R : x_k\in Z and |x_k|\le t for all k\in nS\right\}.\end{equation}.
elements.formula-2411-008: \begin{equation}A_{n,t}:=\left\{\sum\limits_{k\in nS} \frac{x_k}{2^k}\in R : x_k\in Z and |x_k|\le t for all k\in nS\right\}.\end{equation}
elements.sent-2411-014: We will need the following result to show that the set $A_{n,t}$ has Hausdorff dimension zero for every $n,t\in\natn$.
elements.sent-2411-015: For $n\in \mathbb{N}$ and $z\ge 1$, define \begin{equation}g_n(z):=\emph{\#}\{k\in nS: k\le z\}.\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-016: Then we have the bound \begin{equation}g_n(z) \le (2+\log_2 z)^n.\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-017: Clearly, for every $z\ge 1$, we have { \begin{equation}g_n(z)\le g_1(z)^n and g_1(z)\leq 2+log_2z.\end{equation}. The result follows.}
elements.sent-2411-018: The set $A_{n,t}$ has Hausdorff dimension zero for all $n,t\in \mathbb{N}$.
elements.sent-2411-019: Let $x\in nS$ and write \begin{equation}x=\sum\limits_{i=1}^{\infty} \frac{a_i}{2^{k_i}}\end{equation} with $a_i\in \mathbb{Z}$ and $|a_i|\le t$.
elements.sent-2411-020: Pick $j\in \mathbb{N}$ and split $x$ into \begin{equation}x=m_x+e_x,\end{equation} where \begin{equation}m_x:=\sum\limits_{i=1}^{j-1} \frac{a_i}{2^{k_i}} \quad and \quad e_x:=\sum\limits_{i=j}^{\infty} \frac{a_i}{2^{k_i}}.\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-021: Then using the triangle inequality, we have the bound \begin{equation}|e_x|\le t\sum\limits_{i=j}^{\infty} \frac{1}{2^{k_i}}\le t\sum\limits_{h=k_j}^{\infty} \frac{1}{2^h}=\frac{t}{2^{k_j-1}}.\end{equation}. Let $r_j=t/2^{k_j-1}$.
elements.sent-2411-022: It follows that \begin{equation}A_{n,t}\subseteq \bigcup_{m\in X} I_m,\end{equation} where \begin{equation}X:=\left\{\sum\limits_{i=1}^{j-1} \frac{a_i}{2^{k_i}}: a_i\in Z,\ |a_i|\le t\right\}\end{equation} and \begin{equation}I_m:=[m-r_j,m+r_j].\end{equation}.

elements.sent-2411-026: Since $\delta_j\to 0$ as $j\to \infty$, { this implies that $\mathcal H^d(A_{n,t )=0$ where $\mathcal H^d(A_{n,t )$ is the $d$-dimensional Hausdorff measure of $A_{n,t}$.}}}

elements.sent-2411-035: We aim to establish that $A$ is in fact a subring.
elements.sent-2411-036: To this end, we need the following result.
elements.sent-2411-037: Let $n\in \mathbb{N}$ and let $a_n(s)$ be the number of representations of an element $s\in \natn$ as a sum $s=t_1+t_2+\cdots +t_n$ of $n$ elements $t_1,t_2,\ldots,t_n\in S$.

elements.sent-2411-039: Clearly $a_1(s)\le 1$ for every $s\in\natn$ and hence, we may take $c_1:=1$.
elements.sent-2411-040: Now assume that $n\ge 2$ and that there exists $c_{n-1}\in\rear$ such that $a_{n-1 (s)\le c_{n-1}$ for every $s\in \mathbb{N}$.}
elements.sent-2411-041: Let $s\in \natn$ and suppose \begin{equation}s=t_1+\cdots +t_n\end{equation} is a representation of $s$ as a sum of $n$ elements of $S$.
elements.sent-2411-042: Then for at least one $i\in\{1,\ldots,n\}$, we have $t_i\ge s/n$ and hence $t_i\neq 0$.
elements.sent-2411-044: As $s/n\le t_i\le s$, we observe that $\log_2 s-\log_2 n\le k\le \log_2 s$.
elements.sent-2411-045: So if \begin{equation}B_{n,s}:=[\,\log_2 s-\log_2 n,\, \log_2 s\,]\cap \natn_0,\end{equation} then we see that \begin{equation}a_n(s)\le n\sum\limits_{k\in B_{n,s}} a_{n-1}(s-2^k) \le n(1+\log_2 n)c_{n-1}.\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-046: So we are done by taking $c_n:=n(1+\log_2 n)c_{n-1}$.
elements.sent-2411-047: The set $A$ is a a subring of $\mathbb{R}$.
elements.sent-2411-048: It suffices to show that $1\in A$ and that $A$ is closed under multiplication.
elements.sent-2411-050: To establish that $A$ is closed under multiplication, we proceed as follows.. Let $x,y\in A$.
elements.sent-2411-051: There exists $m,n\in \mathbb{N}$ such that $x\in A_m$ and $y\in A_n$.
elements.sent-2411-052: Therefore, \begin{equation}x=\sum\limits_{k\in mS} \frac{x_k}{2^k} \quad and \quad y=\sum\limits_{\ell\in nS} \frac{y_\ell}{2^\ell}\end{equation} where $(x_k)_{k\in mS}$ and $(y_\ell)_{\ell\in nS}$ are bounded sequences of integers.
elements.sent-2411-053: Now \begin{equation}xy=\left(\sum\limits_{k\in mS} \frac{x_k}{2^k}\right) \left(\sum\limits_{\ell\in nS} \frac{y_\ell}{2^\ell}\right)= \sum\limits_{(k,\ell)\in mS\times nS} \frac{x_ky_\ell}{2^{k+\ell}}=\sum\limits_{r\in (m+n)S} \frac{z_r}{2^r}\end{equation} where \begin{equation}z_r:=\sum\limits_{\substack{(k,\ell)\in mS\times nS k+\ell=r}} x_ky_\ell.\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-054: The number of representations of an element $r\in (m+n)S$ as a sum $r=k+\ell$ of elements $k\in mS$ and $\ell\in nS$ is bounded by the number of representations of $r$ which are of the form $r=t_1+t_2+\cdots +t_{m+n}$ where $t_1,t_2,\ldots,t_{m+n}\in S$.
elements.sent-2411-055: Hence, we deduce that the sequence $(z_r)_{r\in (m+n)S}$ is bounded as a consequence of Lemma \ref{representations and the boundedness of the sequences $(x_k)_{k\in mS}$ and $(y_\ell)_{\ell\in nS}$.}
elements.sent-2411-057: This establishes that $A$ is closed under multiplication.

elements.sent-2411-078: The above construction of an uncountable subring of $\mathbb{R}$ of Hausdorff dimension zero goes through if the initial set $S=\{0,1,2,2^2,2^3,\ldots\}$ is replaced by a set $T\cup\{0\}$ where $T$ is a subset of $\natn$ which has the following property: There exists $b\in \rear$ such that for every $n\in\natn$, the cardinality of the set $\{\log t:t\in T\}\cap [n,n+1)$ is less than $b$.
elements.sent-2411-079: We construct a subring as mentioned in the title (hence this subring has Lebesgue measure zero).
elements.sent-2411-080: For a subset $B\subseteq \mathbb R$ we let $d(B)$ denote the diameter of $B$.
elements.sent-2411-081: Given a countable collection of subsets $\mathcal C\subseteq \mathcal P(\mathbb R)$ and $s\geq 0$ we define \begin{equation}d(s,\mathcal C):=\sum_{B\in \mathcal C} d(B)^s.\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-082: We note that $d(s,\mathcal C)\in [0,\infty]$ and does not depend on the ordering of the subsets in the collection $\mathcal C$.
elements.sent-2411-083: For $\delta>0$, we say that a collection of subsets $\mathcal C$ is a {\it $\delta$-cover of a subset $F\subseteq \mathbb R$ if for every $B\in \mathcal C$ we have $d(B)\leq \delta$ and if $F\subseteq \cup\, \mathcal C$.}
elements.sent-2411-084: Given a subset $F\subseteq\mathbb R$ and $s,\delta\in\mathbb R$ with $s\geq 0$, and $\delta >0$, we define \begin{equation}\mathcal H^s_\delta(F)=\inf\big\{d(s,\mathcal C):\mathcal C is a countable $\delta$-cover of $F$\big\}.\end{equation}.
elements.formula-2411-040: \begin{equation}\mathcal H^s_\delta(F)=\inf\big\{d(s,\mathcal C):\mathcal C is a countable $\delta$-cover of $F$\big\}.\end{equation}
elements.sent-2411-085: If $\delta_1<\delta_2$, then we see that $\mathcal H^s_{\delta_1 (F)\geq \mathcal H^s_{\delta_2 (F)$.}}
elements.sent-2411-086: The {\it $s$-dimensional Hausdorff measure $\mathcal H^s(F)$} is defined to be the limit of $\mathcal H_\delta^s(F)$ as $\delta\to 0^+$.
elements.sent-2411-087: It can be shown (see \cite{F2 ) that there exists $\alpha\in [0,1]$ such that for every $s\in [0,\alpha)$ we have $\mathcal H^s(F)=\infty$, and for every $s >\alpha$ we have $\mathcal H^s(F)=0$.}
elements.sent-2411-088: The number $\alpha$ is called the {\it Hausdorff dimension of $F$ and is denoted by $\dim_\mathcal H F$.}
elements.sent-2411-089: If $F=\cup\,\mathcal C$ where $\mathcal C$ is a countable collection of subsets of $\mathbb R$, then $\dim_\mathcal H F=\sup\big\{\dim_\mathcal H A:A\in\mathcal C\big\}$.
elements.sent-2411-090: We give an explicit construction of a proper subring of $\mathbb R$ which is a Borel set.
elements.sent-2411-091: We also show explicitly that it has Hausdorff dimension zero.
elements.sent-2411-092: We let $\mathbb N_0$ denote the set of non-negative integers, $\mathbb N$ denote the set of positive integers, and $\mathbb Z$ denote the set of integers.

elements.sent-2411-150: In 1966, Erd\H{o s and Volkmann \cite{EV showed that for every $d\in [0,1]$ there exists a subgroup of $\mathbb R$ which is Borel measurable and has Hausdorff dimension $d$.}}
elements.sent-2411-152: Davies showed that for every $d\in [0,1]$ there exists a subring of $\mathbb R$ which has Hausdorff dimension $d$ and which is not a Borel set.
elements.sent-2411-153: In \cite{EM , it is shown that every proper subring of $\mathbb R$ which is a Borel set, has Hausdorff dimension zero.}
elements.sent-2411-154: It has come to our attention that our construction has some similarities to that of \cite{EV , but our series expansion of real numbers is inspired by the binary expansion.}
elements.sent-2411-155: Erd\H{o s and Volkmann \cite{EV noted that the question of the Hausdorff dimension of subrings and subfields remained open.}}
elements.sent-2411-156: We feel that it is of interest to see how Lemma \ref{representations is crucially used to show that we get a subring of $\mathbb R$ by using numbers which are represented by special series expansions.}
elements.sent-2411-157: Also, we are able to see relatively easily that the set $A$ has Hausdorff dimension zero.
elements.sent-2411-158: We have been informed of the following construction.
elements.sent-2411-159: If $A$ is a compact Cantor set such that for every $n\in \mathbb N$ the set $A^n$ has Hausdorff dimension zero, then it can be shown that the subring of $\mathbb R$ which is generated by $A$ has Hausdorff dimension zero.
elements.sent-2411-160: However, we are unable to find a reference for the construction of such a set.
elements.sent-2411-161: Our construction is very explicit using series expansions and we use neither the Continuum Hypothesis nor the Axiom of choice.
